speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] with Israel Kipen. Israel, we were talking about your contact with the Jews in the ghetto, and your access to the ghetto. Would you like to continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The occasion for me to go to the ghetto was other formal one. It came about as a result of the Japanese [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] intent of sending all British citizens into camp for the duration of the war, because they were-- they were enemy s-- subjects, being British, and they had to be interned, the same as the Japanese were interned in America. And that included the [INAUDIBLE] and everybody else. It so happened that [INAUDIBLE] from the charitable trust funds built a Jewish day school in [INAUDIBLE] where the German Jews live prior to the ghetto. For that purpose to give Jewish children some form of Jewish education. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when they were due to leave and go to the-- to camp. They had--

speaker: interviewer
text: To the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- not to the ghetto, to camp, to Japanese camp, not the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were British citizens. They were not stateless. Yeah. Then they wanted to hand over this school to some authority because they won't be there. Who will run it? And they turned to the Russian Jewish community to take over the responsibility for that school for the duration, because the Russian Jews were not subject to going to internment because were Russian and stateless, and were not enemy subjects. So they were left unmolested. They were left [INAUDIBLE]. And the Russian Jews said yes, they will do it. And they made a time to go and have a look at the entire place. But there was one little hitch. The Russian Jews knew about Jewish day schools less than I know what's happening on Mars today. And they wanted to take somebody with them who knows-- who knows something about Jewish schools to give them some of-- sort of an idea what it is, and how it is, and so on and so forth. Having been known to them from the management of the kitchen and the hospital, it was a logical thing for them to turn to me and invite me to come with them as part of that investigating [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of events and we inspect the school. It was very nice school. And I had my first lesson in-- in the difference between the concept of European understanding of Judaism and Jewish education and Anglo-Saxon and Jewish education system. I found the class with the children sitting there with their heads covered, and their teacher with his head covered teaching them about a tree and a-- and a bird, and so on and so forth, a [INAUDIBLE] primer. And I stood there, and I looked, and I just couldn't make it out. And when we walked out, the [INAUDIBLE] was with us-- one of the [INAUDIBLE]-- the-- the-- the Shanghai [INAUDIBLE] was with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: As we walked-- he was a tall, towering man, broad shoulders, 6 foot 2'. I turned around to him and said, so, whatever his name was, tell me please, why are the children sitting there with their heads covered? And he said, I don't know. I said, but they are not learning religion, they are not learning bible. That's learning a secular story about birds and trees. It's not Jewish religion. I don't know. The only answer I could give you is that's how it's done in England. So that was my first lesson. When I came later to Melbourne and I had to start fighting for-- for the Jewish content of Mount Scopus College, I already had some inkling what Jewish education in [INAUDIBLE] is. So that is-- that was experience. What I saw at that-- at that ghetto was very, very depressing. What did you witness?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we-- we went-- after we inspected the school, we went to have a look at dormitories. There were people-- some people lived in homes, some people lived in dormitories. That was old warehouses converted totally, the entire area, into two-story bed setups. I mean, the middle of the day, we saw people lying on the beds with nothing to do. Some played cards, some-- it dispirited, without purpose, without hope, without-- without an inkling to what to do. And from what we saw and what we knew was that the higher the people were in their previous life socially, the more well off they were, the most-- the more well-provided they were economically, the further they fell in their new role as refugees. Because they were not prepared. They never had to struggle for daily existence. The shock of that fall from grace, so to speak, in terms of their well-being, was so great that they could never find to-- to stand on their own feet again. Those who didn't have and had to go and-- and make a living could care less. They-- they-- they sold dollars, they sold pins, they sold needles, they sold shaving treatment. Anything is sold because that was natural and normal. Those people who were doctors and lawyers and-- and-- and-- and the upper crust of society, they were completely lost. They just didn't know how to reconstruct their new life forms and so on, and especially in the ghetto, where there were no opportunities.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- it-- it was such a-- if you could have gone outside to work, well, but in the ghetto itself, what could you do? on top of one another, from-- form one another. There was no source of income apart from what-- what was provided there. And a few little cafes and-- or-- or things that some enterprising individuals undertook for those who could afford it, and so on. But people were debilitated in terms of self-respect, self-- of-- of-- of self-worth. And it was written all over them. It was pitiful, pitiful to see. How did they maintain a supply of food in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the joint provided it. And the man-- the man who was managing that enterprise in the ghetto for the-- was a man by the name of Bergman. He was a colleague of mine who after the war-- who after the war when he came to America, he gave testimony to the Senate subcommittee about those years. And he visited me here a few years ago. [INAUDIBLE] was here in Melbourne. And he came to visit in our home. And he was lecture in-- in-- in the-- just some social sciences, whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who supervised or who policed the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Japanese. It was policed-- policed is a very mild word. It was policed with a-- with a particular intent. And I would like to come back [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for one particular aspect that I've omitted, but I would like to put it on record.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the days of the kitchen, when I was running the kitchen in Shanghai, before the ghetto was instituted, one day out of the blue, it was after lunch, about 2 o'clock, somebody came into my office and told me that two Japanese people wanted to see you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I pricked my ears up. That's-- sure enough, they came in. They started questioning is. What is it? What is the gathering here. We are coming here, well then, what is it? And they were explained to me the best way I could, that it is a nonprofit organization that provides a hot meal a day for people who are otherwise would have been starving. And so-- yes, but you are Jews here, all Jews here, aren't you? Yes, we are all Jews. Well, if you are Jews then you must be friends of England because England has got the mandate over Palestine. And yeah, all us Jews want-- are Zionists and want to go to Palestine. The implication is that if you are friends of England, yeah, by implication, friends of an enemy-- the friend of an enemy is your enemy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had to explain to these men [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how-- that some of his basic assumptions are somewhat farfetched. And to tell him that first of all, the people who come here, Jewish politics is the last thing on their mind anyhow. They are concentrating on one purpose only, to keep body and soul together. If they pull through alive and in the reasonable health, they will have achieved the utmost that they can ever hope for to achieve under such depressing and-- and tragic circumstances.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for anybody to-- to-- to-- to get involved in politics, per se, is absurd [INAUDIBLE] here, absolutely. And then I said, if you want to know the sociology of Jewish-- of Jewish involvement in terms of political identification, Zionists those days were only a very small minority of people [INAUDIBLE] a majority of people among the Jews who were against Zionism, who were actively against Zionism, who didn't want-- the assimilationists didn't want to be identified as Jews, let alone as Zionists. The Bundists didn't want to be Zionists because they wanted to leave, and did dispersion, and that was their ideology. So you have got the wrong aspect of approach to the entire issue.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it wasn't an easy way to persuade him. Somehow I disengaged from them. I certainly didn't-- they didn't forget me. I certainly didn't forget them. They were members of the secret police, of course. And it was soon after-- soon after that the ghetto proclamation came in. And the whole thing had to be found out and the matter became-- it came off the agenda, so to speak. If we would have continued to be providing meals there and congregating in that same place month after month and month after month, most probably their interest would have sharpened and the insinuations about how-- about what they would have liked to-- to-- to put onto us all or-- or-- or stick to our backs, so to speak, would have been harder to refute because it was an uneven argument. It was a very uneven argument between-- between the secret police in war-time time situation and helpless refugees who live by the grace of those-- of those occupying forces.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyhow, but when we-- coming back to the ghetto and there is a connection in that respect, the-- the-- the-- there were people-- a special Japanese office controlling the ghetto and the-- the-- the coming and going in and out of the ghetto. People that given permission to go out to work from the ghetto for set number of hours a day, but they had to get back-- was closed at 6 o'clock. They all had to be in, like a-- a medieval city and the gates were shut closed at a certain time. And people used to find nominal employment with-- with-- with the Jewish people or the Chinese people, whoever it is, and so that they could go out [INAUDIBLE] and make some-- something to help them in their daily struggle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but the people-- the two people who were governing that entire system, one of them was named [? Kubota, ?] was the name of the man. And other one's name, I don't remember. I should've-- and they were-- they were the-- the-- the terror of the Jewish community because they were both sadistic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was in front of them, yes. I had to come and face them because they used to call you out from time to time. One of them used to go into unprovoked rage, used to beat people. He didn't beat me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't witness [INAUDIBLE] but I knew people who were just slapped down and kicked, and-- and-- and so-- and certainly threatened. And some people whom he-- he took a personal dislike, or whatever it is, he send them to the-- to the typhoid-infested jails, which was a death sentence. Nobody came out of there. And I knew many people who never came out of those jails. The minute you went-- you were sent to jail by [? Kubota, ?] that was as good as-- as-- as a death sentence. And I've even eulogized one of those victims in Shanghai at an open grave. It was-- that was a very, very harsh and frightening and-- and almost a-- a constant neurosis of the-- of all people inside the ghetto, particularly the Polish Jews because you never knew when you have to confront these people. You never knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue a Jewish existence yourself, working at the hospital? Were you conscious of practicing as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sure. There was a synagogue. The high holidays were observed. In fact, the-- the-- the cantor of the Bialystok Synagogue was with me in Shanghai. And he-- and he lead the prayers at [INAUDIBLE] and that-- [INAUDIBLE] Synagogue-- and it was a sight to see because they've never heard anything like it. And I've heard women coming out of-- the first time [INAUDIBLE] women come, people come to synagogue [INAUDIBLE] people in cars in Bialystok who didn't drive with-- with cars to the synagogue on Yom Kippur. And I hear one woman saying to the other, in Russian she says, how extraordinary. It was like an opera. She never heard anything like it. It was magnificent voice. And so-- and I remember the next day, during the day, a Russian Orthodox priest came and stood in the synagogue to listen to the order of prayer and to the melodies because the Russian Orthodox easter music, you know, is so very magnificent. [INAUDIBLE] Korsakov has-- has included it in his-- in his compositions and so on. And he stood there transfixed and fascinated. Nobody interfered with him. Nobody asked him to sit down, but nobody interfered with him. He stood there with his-- with his staff, with his high thing, and his-- and-- and he stood there for a long time just listening and watching and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the end of quarter was a-- a Jewish club of the Russian Jews there where a social life was going go on in comfort, because the people lived a normal life. They made money during the war, those who were local citizens, and so on. And they live-- lived it up [INAUDIBLE] in which life was understood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was in clubs. There was the French club. There was the British club. And there was the Russian Jewish club. It was-- it was simple as that. I used to frequent that club often, by the nature of my position as head of the kitchen and the hospital. So I had to deal with the leaders of the community so I was privy to their activities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you receive news from overseas about the progress of the war? Were you aware of developments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only in general terms, about the development of war and the different fronts. And of course, after '42 and '43, certain murmurings started to circulate in the community about-- about concentration camps for Jews. But it was so farfetched that we couldn't give it credence. The-- the Jewish agency didn't want to believe it. The-- Dr. Stephen Wise in New York didn't want to believe it. How could we take such things as-- as gospel truths coming at other end of the world from unknown source, or whatever. But the murmurs were there. The murmurs were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship between Jews of different nationalities and different religious orientation?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Shanghai--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Shanghai.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I said before, our contacts with Russian Jews was very friendly, and very easy, very easy. We dealt with one another. Some people came to people's homes together. And of course, when I ran afterwards the hospital and so on, I was very much involved also socially with these people in the sense because of women's committees. And by that time, they knew me from the kitchen years, and so on, so on. So I had a very particular relationship with these people. And as-- as fate would have it-- and the ironies of fate are extraordinary-- the chairman of the committee of the board of the hospital came in '47 here. He had to leave China in-- when Mao came in, '49. He had to leave his real estate behind. And he came to Sydney an old man. And I had to give him an agency of my [INAUDIBLE] products from Melbourne for him to sell in Sydney, to be able to make a living. That's how tables have turned in a short few-- few years. But that was the interaction. And it was of, course, it was an unequal relationship in terms that they were settled and well-to-do. And we were to some extent at the mercy of circumstances and their good will, to the extent to which they were ready to to help us, and so on. But we e existed. Somehow other people existed. Some found work. Some did this. Some did that. I don't think people really went hungry. Somehow-- somehow, they managed. You know, and in normal circumstances how we're all equal in our traditions of life and so on. [INAUDIBLE] all in the same homes, and so it's individual. Some manage better, some [INAUDIBLE]. But as I said before, the people least-- least educated were the ones who adjusted first, precisely because they had no inhibitions. They didn't have to consider how far down they were from what they were previously. They had nothing, and they came with nothing. And every opportunity was an appropriate opportunity for them. And they grabbed it. And they were-- precisely because they didn't have those inhibitions-- that I was yesterday a owner of a big plant and a big enterprise and so on, and employed 200 people or 500 people-- they did-- they did business and were happy with their lot and made Shabbat for themselves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the strongest images of life in Shanghai that you've brought with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the strongest images that I've brought, they were mixed-- it was a mixture of those contrasts. And of course, there was always that element of knowing and realizing that I've got a family somewhere in-- in-- in Kazakhstan. Father-- father going out early in the morning, walking for miles to a potato field and standing in the sun, digging and-- and sowing and then-- and digging up the potatoes because if he wouldn't bring in the potatoes in time, wintertime [INAUDIBLE] with nothing to eat. Nobody-- nobody did them any wrong, but nobody did any good, either. They were on their own. They were send off, go for you life, do what you can. And they had to make the best. And then they managed to somehow scrape money together and buy a cow, and had milk. And milk was not only a commodity to eat, it was a commodity of exchange. For the milk they could get potatoes from somebody else, or they could get something, or they could get a shirt, or could get something like that, and so on. So I've congratulated them on the addition to the family. It was a major addition to the family, a cow. [INAUDIBLE] say that we will be running a cow and to be [INAUDIBLE] such a precious thing. My mother had to go to collect firewood in the wood and bring on -- on her back, carry a bundle of firewood as you see peasant women in Vietnam [INAUDIBLE], and so on. Those were the images. And four-- four young children to be educated there in such circumstances. They were starving at times. They were starving at times. Apart from the uncertainties of the regime, apart from you never knew what happens there next in terms of-- of-- of the communist order. And they were-- they were because anybody who worked for the government was always at the-- at the-- at the peril because nothing in the government offices was 100%. Because if it there was 100%, the offices would never exist, would never tick. So it was always done with some sort of-- of-- combinations of interactions between one office and another, and what-- whatever it is. And if you were the accountant, and one of my brothers was the accountant that-- in that enterprise, you would have been the one who's sent to jail for-- for any-- for any abnormalities that were. And if you couldn't trade these things to keep the wheels running, then you would be kicked out and you had no income [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Israel, when were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Liberated in the-- in the true sense of the word, the technical sense of liberation, does not apply to me in that sense, because nobody came into our camp to-- to open the door and say, you are free. But I was liberated-- inverted commas-- the day of the Japanese surrender, in August '45. I remember that day very clearly. I remember it because when the news came through that the Japanese-- Japanese surrendered, and we knew that it's coming because we could see the-- the attacks on Shanghai wharfs and so on, that the Americans were-- and we knew what's happening, and so on. That day, suddenly the-- the-- the self-- self-confident occupiers suddenly tried to melt into thin air. They walked the street hugging the-- the walls of the buildings, trying not to be noticed, trying not to be seen. It was a picture to be seen and to be-- observe to see what happens over-- withing hours, in the mentality of a people from being the-- the process of force and power and suddenly to become the subject of mercy and-- and-- and and the-- and the potential-- and the potential subject of accountability. That day I remember very clearly, very clearly. And-- and that day, for which we all hoped of course and so on, I knew. Americans immediately came in. And everything, you know, where the American Army came in with the PX stores and so on, and were new opportunities. And I was helping somebody make what-- spirits for them to sell on the market. You didn't need licenses for the government for that. And I helped some other person make soap, and whatever else to do. But that day, I said to myself, OK. And it was long before we knew about Mao Tse-tung. Mao Tse-tung [INAUDIBLE] the long march was three years away, four years in '49. I said to myself, the world is back in place. The Western Allies won the war. I want to get out of here quick, as quick as I can, because I have spent my youth in limbo. I left the home. I was 20. I finished-- by the time I came to Australia I was 27. Not-- not that the years between 17 and 20 were years of-- of-- of joy, and it was-- it was-- we never knew what it is to be free of war and so on. But I've lost seven years of my life. And I knew, instinctively, that I don't want to build my future in an alien cultural environment, no matter what the opportunities financially may be. I don't want the opportunities. I don't want to get stuck at the Far East. I don't want it. I want to live in a European society. That was my only-- and of course, at the time I wanted to go to Palestine. Because I left home with the idea of going to Palestine. And I've sent my Warsaw University index from Vilna to the Hebrew University in preparation for the ultimate arrival to Palestine to do my studies there, which of course, I don't know that they've ever received.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. That's the end of tape seven.
